Q1: I tried to keep a consistent study routine, honestly. I would block out time in the afternoons, usually around 3-4 hours each day, and I'd go through the lecture materials systematically. I'm someone who needs to hear things to understand them, so I'd often play recordings while taking notes. Even when I wasn't feeling great emotionally, I'd still sit down and go through the motions because that's just what I do.
Q2: I tried to keep things regular, but I'd be lying if I said deadlines didn't shift things around sometimes. Most weeks I had a set schedule, but when assignments were due, I'd extend my study time or rearrange other things. The routine helped me feel in control even when I was stressed or feeling down, so I clung to it pretty tightly.
Q3: Usually it was just habit—I'd log in after lunch as part of my routine. Sometimes there'd be a notification about an assignment or a deadline reminder that prompted me. Other times I logged in just to check if anything new was posted, even if I didn't end up doing much once I was there. It became almost automatic after a while.
Q4: Yes, definitely. Around the third week, logging in just became part of my daily schedule, like checking email. I set a specific time for it and stuck to it most days. I think having the gamified tasks helped a bit because there were points and progress bars that made me want to check in, even if my actual engagement wasn't always deep.
Q5: The audio lectures and recorded sessions were most useful for me since I learn best by listening. I'd often replay sections multiple times until concepts clicked. The discussion posts were somewhat helpful too because hearing other perspectives sometimes clarified things I was confused about. The gamified tasks gave me small wins that kept me going through harder weeks.
Q6: I mostly ignored the reading materials and PDFs. I know they were probably important, but reading dense text just doesn't work for my brain the way listening does. I'd skim them sometimes or download them with good intentions, but they'd just sit in my folders. The peer interaction features I used minimally too—I participated enough to meet requirements but didn't go beyond that.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it directly connected to what would be on exams or assignments. If I could see how it would help my grade, I'd engage with it. The gamified elements also made things feel worthwhile because earning points or completing challenges gave me a small sense of accomplishment, which I needed during the times I was feeling sad.
Q8: Optional activities felt like extras I couldn't afford to spend energy on. With eleven courses, I was already stretched thin, so I prioritized required work. Required activities had clear consequences, which motivated me even when I didn't feel like doing them. Optional ones felt nice in theory but I rarely had the mental energy to pursue them.
Q9: Yes, quite a lot actually. I'd download audio files and listen to them on my phone while doing other things around my apartment. I also used external YouTube videos to supplement topics I didn't understand well. My notes from listening to lectures were extensive, and I'd review those instead of logging back into the LMS repeatedly.
Q10: There were many evenings where I'd study from my printed notes or listen to downloaded lectures for hours, but the LMS wouldn't capture any of that. I'd also sometimes study with a friend over the phone, talking through concepts together. None of that showed up anywhere in my activity logs, but it was real learning time.
Q11: Absolutely. I'd watch a video once on the LMS, download the audio version, and then listen to it multiple times offline over the next few days. It was more convenient for me to study that way, especially when I wanted to listen while doing chores or walking. The LMS only saw that initial access, not the hours I spent with the material afterward.
Q12: There were definitely harder periods, usually when the sadness hit harder than usual. Some weeks I felt like I was just going through the motions without really absorbing anything. When multiple assignments piled up or when I hadn't attended classes in a while, catching up felt overwhelming. The stress built up even though I tried to maintain my routine.
Q13: The workload from having eleven online courses was honestly brutal sometimes. I'd feel fatigued and my concentration would suffer. When I was stressed or feeling low, I'd still log in and complete tasks, but it was mechanical—I wasn't really thinking deeply. Sometimes I'd just click through things to maintain my completion rate without truly engaging.
Q14: Yes, this happened more than I'd like to admit. I'd have videos playing but my mind would be elsewhere, thinking about other assignments or just feeling numb. I'd technically watch the whole thing, the system would mark it complete, but I'd realize afterward I couldn't remember what was covered. It felt like I was present but not really there.
Q15: I postponed discussion posts a lot because they required more active thinking than I had energy for. Even though I knew participation mattered, I'd put it off until the last minute. The same with some assignments—I'd know they were important but the sadness made it hard to start. Once I started, I could usually finish, but starting was the hardest part.
Q16: Deadlines basically dictated my LMS activity spikes. I'd be more consistent in the days leading up to due dates, logging in more frequently and spending more time on the platform. Without deadlines, I might have let things slide more. They were stressful but also necessary to keep me on track when my motivation wasn't enough on its own.
Q17: Feedback had a mixed effect on me. Positive feedback gave me a small boost and made me want to engage more, but critical feedback sometimes made me feel worse about myself. I'd try to use it constructively, but when I was already feeling sad, it was harder to take. I noticed I'd engage more after receiving good scores on gamified tasks.
Q18: The course structure helped because it gave me clear expectations and deadlines to follow. Having a routine to comply with was actually comforting when everything else felt uncertain. But the structure also meant a lot of required activities that felt like boxes to check rather than meaningful learning experiences. It kept me moving forward even when I didn't feel like it.
Q19: The gamified elements made engagement easier because they broke things into smaller, achievable goals. That helped when I felt overwhelmed. However, the heavy reliance on reading materials made things harder for me as an auditory learner. More audio options or recorded explanations would have helped me engage more deeply without struggling so much.
Q20: I think those students might be like me on my worse days—logging in, clicking around, maybe watching videos without really absorbing them, but then not having the energy or focus to actually complete assignments well. High activity doesn't mean high-quality engagement. Sometimes you're just going through motions to feel like you're doing something.
Q21: That actually resonates with me somewhat. My attendance was really low, but I still managed to pass because I studied a lot offline. I'd download materials and work through them on my own time, away from the LMS. Someone looking at my logs might think I wasn't engaged, but I was putting in the hours—just not in ways the system could see.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns like login frequency, time spent, and completion rates. They're good at tracking what you clicked on and when. But they miss the quality of engagement—whether you actually understood something or were just going through motions. They can't capture offline studying, emotional state, or whether the learning actually stuck with you.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about and understanding the material, not just completing tasks. It's when I'm genuinely thinking about concepts, making connections, and feeling curious rather than just checking boxes. For me, true engagement is rare—most of the time I'm complying with requirements rather than deeply invested. But when it happens, it feels different.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I struggled emotionally throughout the course, or how much effort it took just to maintain my routine when I was feeling sad. It wouldn't capture the hours I spent listening to downloaded lectures or studying from my notes. It also wouldn't show that sometimes my high completion rate was just compliance, not genuine engagement or understanding.
